seoTitle: How to Install and Set Up an SSL Certificate From GoDaddy
metaDescription: Learn how to install, activate, and connect an SSL certificate from GoDaddy to your website domain, and fix a Not Secure warning by enabling HTTPS.
intro: A website showing a Not Secure warning usually means its SSL certificate isn't active yet, and GoDaddy makes it possible to fix that whether you bought a standalone certificate or got one free with hosting. This guide covers requesting and activating the certificate, verifying domain ownership, and connecting it through cPanel, GoDaddy DNS, or a third-party host. By the end, your site should be serving over HTTPS.

steps.heading: Request and activate your SSL certificate
steps.body: Start by requesting your SSL certificate in GoDaddy, whether it's a paid certificate or one included free with your hosting plan.

steps.heading: Verify domain ownership
steps.body: Complete the domain verification step so GoDaddy can confirm you control the domain before issuing the certificate.

steps.heading: Connect the certificate to your hosting
steps.body: Depending on your setup, install the certificate through cPanel, GoDaddy DNS, or your third-party hosting provider's control panel.

steps.heading: Confirm HTTPS is working
steps.body: Reload your site and check that the Not Secure warning is gone and HTTPS is active to build visitor trust and support SEO.

faq.question: Do I get a free SSL certificate with GoDaddy hosting?
faq.answer: Many GoDaddy hosting plans include a free SSL certificate, though you can also purchase a standalone one.

faq.question: Why does my site still show Not Secure after buying SSL?
faq.answer: This usually means the certificate hasn't been fully installed or connected to your hosting yet, so double-check the activation and DNS steps.

faq.question: Can I install SSL through cPanel?
faq.answer: Yes, cPanel is one of the supported ways to install and manage your SSL certificate alongside GoDaddy DNS.

faq.question: Does adding SSL actually help SEO?
faq.answer: Enabling HTTPS is considered a positive signal for search engines and helps build visitor trust, alongside its security benefits.
